research.title: Claude Fable 5 for Developers — Anthropic's New Mythos-Class Model, Hands-On
research.whyTrending: Anthropic shipped Claude Fable 5 (claude-fable-5) on June 9, 2026 — first Claude 5 family model, new Mythos-class tier above Opus. Suspended June 12 - July 1 due to US export controls after an Amazon researcher jailbreak; redeployed with stricter classifiers that cause false-positive refusals on systems-level coding. Breaking API changes vs Opus: always-on thinking, HTTP 200 refusals, 30-day retention requirement, $10/$50 pricing.

seo.suggestedTitle: Claude Fable 5 Review: What Breaks When You Migrate

gaps: Nobody shows working refusal-handling code (stop_reason check + server-side fallbacks parameter)
No coverage of the monitoring blind spot: HTTP 200 refusals invisible to error-rate dashboards
Launch recaps ignore the June 12 - July 1 suspension and its architectural lesson (don't hard-pin one model)
No honest cost math: when $10/$50 beats $5/$25 Opus 4.8 and when it doesn't
No guidance on when NOT to use Fable 5 (routine work, security-adjacent code, ZDR orgs)
strengths: DataCamp has a runnable end-to-end tutorial structure
dev.to post has a concise breaking-changes list

officialSources.note: Launch post: Mythos-class tier, safeguards, benchmarks, pricing
Export controls June 12, redeployment July 1, stricter classifiers

keyFacts: Released June 9, 2026; model ID claude-fable-5; GA on Claude API, Claude Platform on AWS, Bedrock, Google Cloud, Microsoft Foundry
Mythos-class tier above Opus; Fable 5 and Mythos 5 share the same underlying model; Mythos 5 is Project Glasswing only (defensive cybersecurity)
Pricing $10/$50 per MTok (vs Opus 4.8 $5/$25); 1M context window (default), 128K max output
Thinking always on: thinking {type: disabled} and budget_tokens both return 400; omit the thinking param; control depth with output_config.effort (low..xhigh, max)
Raw chain of thought never returned; display: summarized gives a readable summary, omitted (default) gives empty thinking text
Safety classifiers target offensive cyber, bio/chem, and reasoning extraction; refusals return HTTP 200 with stop_reason refusal and stop_details.category
Server-side fallbacks: betas [server-side-fallback-2026-06-01] + fallbacks [{model: claude-opus-4-8}] re-serves declined requests in the same call
Requires 30-day data retention; ZDR orgs get 400 invalid_request_error on every request
No assistant prefill (same as 4.6+ family); same tokenizer as Opus 4.8
SWE-bench Verified ~95% (vals.ai, vs Opus 4.8 88.6%); SWE-Bench Pro 80.3% on Anthropic scaffolding (contested); best score on Cognition FrontierCode
Stripe used it for a 50M-line Ruby migration; completed Pokemon FireRed vision-only

June 12: US export controls after Amazon researchers found a vulnerability-exploitation jailbreak; access suspended globally; restored July 1 with classifiers blocking the technique in >99% of cases at the cost of more false positives
Post-redeploy complaints: systems-level code (C/C++/Rust, Win32, words like 'security', 'unsafe', 'hook') can trigger fallback to Opus 4.8
Safeguards trigger in <5% of sessions on average; 1,000+ hours external red-teaming, no universal jailbreaks found
commonProblems: Error dashboards monitoring 4xx/5xx report 100% success while requests are being refused (refusals are HTTP 200)
Code reading response.content[0] unconditionally breaks on pre-output refusals (empty content array)
ZDR orgs debugging payloads when the 400 is actually an org-level retention setting
Security-adjacent legit work triggering false-positive classifier refusals
statistics: Pricing 2x Opus 4.8 on both input and output

strategy.angle: Not a launch recap — a production migration story: refusals that look like success (HTTP 200), a three-week suspension that taught model-pinning lessons, and honest math on when 2x Opus pricing pays off
strategy.title: Claude Fable 5 Review: What Breaks When You Migrate
strategy.excerpt: Claude Fable 5 refused my requests with HTTP 200s, disappeared for three weeks, and still earned a place in my stack. What breaks, how to handle refusals, and when Opus 4.8 is still the right call.
hooks: Monitoring blind spot: my dashboard showed 100% success while Fable 5 was refusing requests — refusals are HTTP 200, not errors
The model got suspended by US export controls three days after launch — a first in the industry
Contrarian: the most capable model on the market should NOT be your default model
outline: Hook: the dashboard that lied (HTTP 200 refusals)
What Fable 5 actually is (Mythos-class, shared model with Mythos 5, Project Glasswing)
The numbers: benchmarks that matter and the ones that are contested
Migration from Opus 4.8: the three things that break (thinking config, prefill, ZDR)
Handling refusals properly: stop_reason, stop_details, server-side fallbacks (working code)
The three-week suspension and what it taught me about model pinning
When to use Fable 5 (and when to stay on Opus 4.8)
